steps.explanation: We start with the product on the left and expand it, aiming to reach the constant $1$ on the right.

steps.description: Recognise a difference of two squares
steps.explanation: The two brackets are a sum and a difference of the same pair, so they multiply as a difference of two squares and the cross terms will cancel.
Multiplying out all four terms shows the two middle terms $-\sec\theta\tan\theta$ and $+\tan\theta\sec\theta$ are equal and opposite.
The middle terms cancel exactly, leaving the clean difference $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta$.
To handle $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta$ we need a link between them, which comes from the fundamental Pythagorean identity.
Dividing every term by $\cos^2\theta$ converts each piece into a reciprocal-function square.
Since $\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta}=\tan^2\theta$ and $\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\theta}=\sec^2\theta$, this is the standard identity $1+\tan^2\theta\equiv\sec^2\theta$.
Subtracting $\tan^2\theta$ from both sides isolates exactly the expression we reached after expanding, and it equals $1$.
Testing at $45^\circ$ gives $1$, matching the right-hand side and supporting the proof.
The product simplifies to $1$ using the difference of squares and the identity $1+\tan^2\theta\equiv\sec^2\theta$, so the identity holds.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Expanding gives $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta$, and since $1+\tan^2\theta\equiv\sec^2\theta$ we have $\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta=1$, so $(\sec\theta+\tan\theta)(\sec\theta-\tan\theta)\equiv1$.
